quizQuestion: Which two Bell Labs engineers created Unix in 1969, writing the OS that would directly influence Linux, macOS, Android, and virtually every modern operating system?
explanation: Ken Thompson and Dennis Ritchie created Unix in 1969 at Bell Labs (AT&T). It was originally written in assembly language for the PDP-7 and rewritten in C around 1973. Unix introduced foundational concepts like a hierarchical file system, pipes, the shell, and plain-text configuration that shaped all subsequent OS design.
visualDescription: A 1969-era Bell Labs office with two engineers at a PDP-7 terminal, the word 'UNIX' scratched on a chalkboard behind them

quizQuestion: Who was the 21-year-old Finnish university student who announced he was building a free hobby OS in August 1991, posting 'it won't be big and professional like gnu'?
explanation: Linus Torvalds announced the Linux kernel on August 25, 1991, famously describing it as 'just a hobby, won't be big and professional like gnu' in a post to the comp.os.minix newsgroup. Version 0.01 followed on September 17. Today Linux powers 97% of the world's top 500 supercomputers and most web servers.
visualDescription: A 1991-era university computer terminal showing the famous newsgroup post announcing Linux, a penguin mascot sketched in the corner

quizQuestion: In what year did Linus Torvalds announce the Linux kernel in his famous 'just a hobby' newsgroup post?
explanation: Linus Torvalds posted his announcement to comp.os.minix on August 25, 1991. The first version (0.01) was released September 17, 1991. Version 1.0 followed in March 1994. Licensed under GNU GPL v2, Linux became the backbone of the open-source software world.
visualDescription: A timeline of the early 1990s with 1991 highlighted, showing the Usenet post that started the Linux revolution

quizQuestion: Who created CP/M in 1974 — the first widely-used OS for personal microcomputers — and is sometimes called 'the man who could have been Bill Gates'?
explanation: Gary Kildall created CP/M (Control Program for Microcomputers) in 1974 for Intel 8080-based systems at Digital Research Inc. When IBM needed an OS for the PC, they approached Microsoft instead of Kildall. Microsoft licensed 86-DOS (essentially a CP/M clone) and delivered MS-DOS — a decision that made Bill Gates a billionaire.
visualDescription: A vintage 1970s microcomputer running CP/M with a command prompt, and a 'what if' split-screen showing two diverging corporate histories

quizQuestion: In what year was MS-DOS released as the operating system for the original IBM PC, beginning Microsoft's domination of personal computing?
explanation: MS-DOS was released in 1981 as the OS for the original IBM PC. Microsoft acquired the underlying 86-DOS from Tim Paterson at Seattle Computer Products for around $50,000 and licensed it to IBM. This deal, not software brilliance, was the foundation of Microsoft's rise. MS-DOS dominated personal computing through the 1980s.
visualDescription: An original IBM PC from 1981 with a blinking C:\ prompt on the screen, a floppy disk labeled 'MS-DOS 1.0' inserted

quizQuestion: In what year did Apple release the original Macintosh — the first commercially successful personal computer with a graphical user interface?
explanation: The original Macintosh was released January 24, 1984, making it the first commercially successful personal computer with a GUI. The GUI concept came from Xerox PARC, which Steve Jobs visited in 1979. The Mac used a mouse-driven desktop with icons and windows. Modern macOS is built on Darwin (BSD Unix), a complete architectural rebuild starting with Mac OS X in 2001.
visualDescription: The iconic original 1984 Macintosh with its small square screen showing a desktop, a mouse sitting beside it

quizQuestion: Who founded Android Inc. in 2003 — the company Google acquired for ~$50 million in 2005 and turned into the world's most-used mobile operating system?
explanation: Andy Rubin co-founded Android Inc. in 2003. Google acquired it in 2005 for approximately $50 million. The first commercial Android device was the HTC Dream (T-Mobile G1), released September 23, 2008. Android is based on the Linux kernel and has over 70% global smartphone market share.
visualDescription: The original HTC Dream phone from 2008 with a keyboard sliding out, the Android robot mascot visible on the screen

quizQuestion: Before Apple renamed it 'iOS' in 2010 when the iPad launched, what was Apple's mobile operating system originally called?
explanation: Apple's mobile OS was originally called 'iPhone OS' when it launched with the first iPhone on June 29, 2007. It was renamed 'iOS' in 2010 when the iPad was released, as the name needed to cover more than just phones. iOS is based on Darwin (BSD Unix), the same foundation as macOS.
visualDescription: The original iPhone from 2007 showing the classic iOS 1.0 interface, with 'iPhone OS' labeled below and an arrow transforming to 'iOS' in 2010

quizQuestion: ARPANET sent its very first message on October 29, 1969 — but the system crashed before the word 'login' was complete. What two letters were actually received?
explanation: On October 29, 1969, Charley Kline at UCLA tried to log in to a computer at SRI International over ARPANET, typing 'login'. The system crashed after transmitting 'l' and 'o'. So the first message ever sent over what would become the internet was the accidental transmission: 'lo'. The network was restored an hour later and the full login succeeded.
visualDescription: A vintage 1969 terminal showing 'lo' on its screen mid-transmission, with a crash error below — and a caption: 'First message on the internet'

quizQuestion: In what year did ARPANET send its first message, establishing the predecessor to the modern internet?
explanation: ARPANET sent its first message on October 29, 1969, from UCLA to SRI International. The network was developed by ARPA (Advanced Research Projects Agency, now DARPA) and was the first operational packet-switching network. By 1990 when it was decommissioned, ARPANET had 213 hosts and had proven the viability of the internet concept.
visualDescription: A 1969-era map of the United States with the four original ARPANET nodes highlighted: UCLA, SRI, UCSB, and University of Utah

quizQuestion: Often called the 'two fathers of the internet,' which duo published the TCP/IP paper in 1974 that defined how all internet communications work?
explanation: Vint Cerf and Bob Kahn published 'A Protocol for Packet Network Intercommunication' in 1974, defining the TCP/IP protocol suite. ARPANET formally switched to TCP/IP on January 1, 1983 — often called the 'birthday of the internet.' Cerf and Kahn received the ACM Turing Award in 2004 for this work.
visualDescription: Two engineers at 1974 computer terminals with diagrams of packet-switching networks, labeled 'Cerf' and 'Kahn', handshaking over a network diagram

quizQuestion: Who invented Ethernet in 1973 at Xerox PARC, went on to co-found 3Com, and named his invention after the 19th-century concept of a light-carrying medium?
explanation: Robert Metcalfe (with David Boggs) invented Ethernet at Xerox PARC in 1973. Metcalfe's design memo is dated May 22, 1973. The name 'Ethernet' comes from 'luminiferous ether' — the now-discredited 19th-century concept of the medium carrying light. Metcalfe co-founded 3Com in 1979 to commercialize Ethernet.
visualDescription: A 1973 Xerox PARC memo sketch showing the original Ethernet bus topology, with the word 'Ether-net' written in architect's lettering

quizQuestion: Packet switching was independently developed by Paul Baran (1964) and a British physicist in 1965. Who coined the actual term 'packet' that we still use today?
explanation: Donald Davies at the UK National Physical Laboratory coined the term 'packet' in 1965. Paul Baran at RAND Corporation had independently developed the concept of breaking data into blocks in 1964. Together their work formed the theoretical foundation of ARPANET and the modern internet.
visualDescription: A data stream being broken into labeled 'packets' numbered 1-5, each taking different routes across a network map before reassembling at the destination

quizQuestion: Who created the Domain Name System (DNS) in 1983 — the 'phone book of the internet' that translates domain names into IP addresses?
explanation: Paul Mockapetris designed DNS in 1983, defining it in RFC 882 and RFC 883. DNS translates human-readable domain names (like google.com) into machine-readable IP addresses. It is a hierarchical distributed database — not a single central lookup — organized into root servers, TLD servers (.com, .org), and authoritative servers.
visualDescription: A browser address bar with 'google.com' being resolved through a DNS hierarchy of servers into a numeric IP address

quizQuestion: Who created HTTP at CERN in 1991 as part of the World Wide Web, giving the internet the protocol that powers every webpage you load?
explanation: Tim Berners-Lee created HTTP at CERN in 1991 as a core component of the World Wide Web. HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) is the application-layer protocol for transmitting HTML documents and other hypermedia. HTTP/0.9 was the first version; HTTP/1.1 became the standard by 1997. HTTPS adds TLS encryption.
visualDescription: CERN's campus in 1991 with a computer screen showing the world's first webpage, the HTTP request/response cycle diagrammed above

quizQuestion: Wi-Fi is a brand name — what is the underlying technical standard ratified in 1997 that all Wi-Fi devices must comply with?
explanation: Wi-Fi is based on IEEE 802.11, first ratified in 1997. The Wi-Fi Alliance was formed in 1999 and branded the technology 'Wi-Fi.' Despite common belief, Wi-Fi does NOT stand for 'Wireless Fidelity' — it is a marketing term. The original standard offered 2 Mbit/s; modern Wi-Fi 6E (802.11ax) reaches ~9.6 Gbit/s.
visualDescription: A wireless router with the Wi-Fi logo and beneath it the technical label 'IEEE 802.11', signal waves radiating outward

quizQuestion: IPv4 addresses have a fixed bit length that limits the internet to roughly 4.3 billion unique addresses — how many bits is an IPv4 address?
explanation: IPv4 addresses are 32 bits long (defined in RFC 791, 1981), giving 2³² = approximately 4.3 billion possible addresses. This pool was exhausted by IANA in February 2011. IPv6 addresses are 128 bits, providing ~340 undecillion addresses. IPv5 was an experimental streaming protocol that was never publicly deployed.
visualDescription: A dotted-decimal IPv4 address (e.g. 192.168.1.1) with its 32-bit binary representation below, contrasted with a longer 128-bit IPv6 address

quizQuestion: Which British mathematician at Bletchley Park developed the Bombe machine to break Nazi Germany's Enigma cipher at scale during WWII?
explanation: Alan Turing (with Gordon Welchman) developed the Bombe at Bletchley Park to crack Enigma messages at scale. Polish mathematicians had first broken Enigma in 1932, but Turing's improvements enabled systematic decryption. Breaking Enigma is estimated to have shortened WWII by 2-4 years. Turing was later prosecuted for homosexuality and died in 1954.
visualDescription: The Bombe machine — a large cabinet of spinning rotors — at Bletchley Park, with intercepted Enigma messages stacked beside it

quizQuestion: In what year did Whitfield Diffie and Martin Hellman publish 'New Directions in Cryptography,' introducing the first publicly known public-key cryptography protocol?
explanation: Diffie and Hellman published 'New Directions in Cryptography' in 1976 — the founding paper of public-key cryptography. Their key exchange allows two parties to establish a shared secret over an insecure channel without prior contact. GCHQ's Malcolm Williamson had independently discovered it in 1974, but that remained classified until 1997. They received the Turing Award in 2015.
visualDescription: Two people exchanging colored paint mixtures in a public demonstration of the Diffie-Hellman key exchange, arriving at the same secret color without revealing it

quizQuestion: Who created PGP (Pretty Good Privacy) in 1991 and was investigated by the US government for 3 years for exporting strong encryption as free software?
explanation: Phil Zimmermann released PGP (Pretty Good Privacy) in 1991 as free software for email encryption. The US government investigated him from 1993-1996 for violating export restrictions on cryptography — treating encryption software as munitions. The case was dropped in 1996. The OpenPGP standard (RFC 4880) is its modern open version.
visualDescription: A 1991 computer screen showing PGP encryption in action with public/private keys, with a US government 'MUNITIONS' warning stamped across it

quizQuestion: SSL (Secure Sockets Layer) — the predecessor to TLS that secures HTTPS today — was created by which browser company in 1995?
explanation: SSL was created by Netscape Communications. SSL 2.0 was released in 1995 as part of Netscape Navigator to secure e-commerce transactions. TLS (Transport Layer Security) replaced SSL; TLS 1.0 was defined in RFC 2246 (1999). The current standard is TLS 1.3 (RFC 8446, 2018). HTTPS = HTTP over TLS.
visualDescription: A 1995 Netscape Navigator browser window with the padlock icon in the corner showing a secure HTTPS connection, the SSL handshake diagrammed above

quizQuestion: In what year did the Morris Worm — the first internet worm to gain widespread attention — infect ~10% of all machines on the internet?
explanation: The Morris Worm was released November 2, 1988 by Cornell University graduate student Robert Tappan Morris. It infected approximately 6,000 machines (~10% of the internet at the time), exploiting vulnerabilities in Unix sendmail, fingerd, and rsh/rexec. Morris was the first person convicted under the Computer Fraud and Abuse Act. The incident led directly to the creation of CERT.
visualDescription: A 1988 network map of the early internet with infected nodes lighting up like a spreading fire, a Cornell University banner in the background

quizQuestion: Who released the first internet worm in 1988, became the first person convicted under the Computer Fraud and Abuse Act, and later co-founded Y Combinator?
explanation: Robert Tappan Morris released the Morris Worm on November 2, 1988 while a Cornell University graduate student. He became the first person convicted under the Computer Fraud and Abuse Act (1986). Later in life, Morris co-founded Viaweb (acquired by Yahoo!) and was a co-founder of Y Combinator, the startup accelerator.
visualDescription: A split biography image: left shows a 1988 Cornell student at a terminal with court documents, right shows a Y Combinator logo

quizQuestion: In what year did the pseudonymous Satoshi Nakamoto publish the Bitcoin whitepaper, introducing blockchain and decentralized digital currency to the world?
explanation: The Bitcoin whitepaper 'Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System' was published on October 31, 2008 by Satoshi Nakamoto — a pseudonym whose real identity remains unknown. The genesis block (Block 0) was mined January 3, 2009. Bitcoin introduced blockchain: a distributed, append-only, cryptographically-linked ledger.
visualDescription: A Halloween-dated 2008 document titled 'Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System' with a mysterious signature 'Satoshi Nakamoto'

quizQuestion: What does the acronym TCP stand for — the connection-oriented protocol that ensures reliable, ordered delivery of data across the internet?
explanation: TCP stands for Transmission Control Protocol. Together with IP (Internet Protocol), it forms TCP/IP — the foundational protocol suite of the internet. TCP provides reliable, ordered, error-checked delivery by breaking data into segments, numbering them, and retransmitting lost ones. Defined in RFC 793 (1981).
visualDescription: A diagram of a TCP connection handshake — SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK — with the letters T, C, P expanding into their full words below

quizQuestion: What does UDP stand for — the fast but fire-and-forget protocol used for video streaming, DNS, and online gaming where speed beats reliability?
explanation: UDP stands for User Datagram Protocol (RFC 768, 1980). Unlike TCP, UDP sends packets without establishing a connection or guaranteeing delivery, order, or error correction. This makes it much faster — ideal for DNS queries, video streaming, VoIP, and online gaming where occasional packet loss is acceptable but latency is critical.
visualDescription: Packets being fired off like arrows with no acknowledgment, contrasted with TCP's careful handshake — both labeled with their speeds and trade-offs

quizQuestion: What does FTP stand for — the 1971 standard protocol for transferring files between a client and a server over a network?
explanation: FTP stands for File Transfer Protocol. Defined in RFC 959 (1985), FTP is one of the oldest internet protocols, standardized in 1971. It allows uploading and downloading files between client and server. Because it transmits credentials in plaintext, SFTP (SSH-secured) and FTPS (TLS-secured) are now preferred for security.
visualDescription: A file directory tree on the left (local) with arrows moving files to a server directory tree on the right, labeled 'FTP'

quizQuestion: What does SSH stand for — the 1995 protocol that replaced Telnet for secure remote login and command execution over an insecure network?
explanation: SSH stands for Secure Shell. Created in 1995 by Tatu Ylönen at Helsinki University of Technology after a password-sniffing attack on his university network, SSH encrypts all communications. It replaced insecure protocols like Telnet and rsh. SSH runs on port 22 by default and is the standard tool for remote server administration.
visualDescription: A terminal window showing an SSH login session to a remote server, the connection marked as encrypted with a padlock icon

quizQuestion: What does SMTP stand for — the 1982 protocol that routes every email you send from server to server across the internet?
explanation: SMTP stands for Simple Mail Transfer Protocol (RFC 821, 1982). It is the standard protocol for sending email between mail servers and from email clients to outgoing mail servers. SMTP handles sending only; POP3 and IMAP handle receiving. SMTP servers typically use port 25 (server-to-server) or port 587 (client-to-server).
visualDescription: An email journey diagram: a user's laptop → SMTP server → internet cloud → recipient's SMTP server → inbox, each step labeled

quizQuestion: What does DNS stand for — the 'phone book of the internet' that translates google.com into an IP address your computer can route to?
explanation: DNS stands for Domain Name System. Created by Paul Mockapetris in 1983, it is a hierarchical distributed database that maps human-readable domain names to IP addresses. Without DNS, you would need to remember numeric IP addresses for every website. DNS queries are typically sent over UDP port 53.
visualDescription: A browser's address bar with 'wikipedia.org' transforming into an IP address '208.80.154.224' via a DNS lookup chain of servers

quizQuestion: What does RAM stand for — the volatile memory that temporarily stores your running programs and loses all data when power is removed?
explanation: RAM stands for Random Access Memory. It is volatile (loses data when power is removed) and stores the data and program instructions currently in use by the CPU. 'Random access' means any memory location can be read at the same speed, unlike sequential-access storage. DRAM (Dynamic RAM) is the most common type in computers.
visualDescription: A RAM stick with lightning bolt symbols showing fast read/write access to any cell, and a power plug being pulled with data disappearing

quizQuestion: What does CPU stand for — the primary component of a computer that executes program instructions and is often called the 'brain' of the computer?
explanation: CPU stands for Central Processing Unit. It fetches, decodes, and executes program instructions. Modern CPUs have multiple cores (enabling parallel execution) and include caches to minimize memory access latency. The Intel 4004 (1971) was the first single-chip microprocessor and pioneered the era of programmable CPUs.
visualDescription: A modern CPU chip with multiple cores labeled, heat spreader on top, socket pins below, arrows showing the fetch-decode-execute cycle

quizQuestion: What does GPU stand for — the term coined by NVIDIA in 1999 for a massively parallel processor originally designed for rendering graphics but now powering AI?
explanation: GPU stands for Graphics Processing Unit, a term coined by NVIDIA in 1999 for the GeForce 256. GPUs have thousands of smaller cores optimized for parallel computation. Originally designed for rendering 3D graphics in video games, GPUs became the dominant hardware for training AI/ML models due to their massive parallel throughput.
visualDescription: A GPU board with thousands of tiny CUDA cores visualized as a grid, running both a video game render and a neural network training task simultaneously

correctAnswer: American Standard Code for Information Interchange
quizQuestion: What does ASCII stand for — the 1963 7-bit character encoding standard that assigns numeric codes to letters, digits, and symbols, and is the basis for Unicode?
explanation: ASCII stands for American Standard Code for Information Interchange, published in 1963 as ASA X3.4-1963. It uses 7-bit encoding to represent 128 characters: 33 control characters and 95 printable characters (letters, digits, punctuation). Unicode is a superset of ASCII, maintaining the same code points for the original 128 characters.
visualDescription: An ASCII table showing letters A-Z with their numeric codes 65-90, and a Unicode table beside it showing the vastly expanded character set built on top
distractors: Automated Standard Coding for Information Interchange
American Symbol Code for Information Interchange
Advanced Standard Code for Information Interchange
ASCII Standard Coding for Internal Interchange
Automated Symbolic Code for Internal Interface
American Sequential Code for Information Interchange
Applied Standard Code for Information Interchange

quizQuestion: What does USB stand for — the 1996 interface standard invented by Intel's Ajay Bhatt that replaced a jungle of connectors and now charges your phone?
explanation: USB stands for Universal Serial Bus. USB 1.0 was released in January 1996, developed by a consortium led by Intel engineer Ajay Bhatt. USB replaced the chaos of serial ports, parallel ports, PS/2, and proprietary connectors with a single universal standard. Modern USB-C also delivers up to 240W of charging power.
visualDescription: A mess of 1990s PC connectors (serial, parallel, PS/2) on the left, replaced by a single clean USB-C port on the right

quizQuestion: What does BIOS stand for — the firmware Gary Kildall named in 1975 that initializes your PC hardware at boot before handing off to the operating system?
explanation: BIOS stands for Basic Input/Output System, a term coined by Gary Kildall in CP/M in 1975. It is firmware stored on a motherboard chip that runs first when a computer powers on, initializing hardware (RAM, storage, peripherals) and loading the OS bootloader. BIOS has been largely replaced by UEFI (Unified Extensible Firmware Interface) in modern computers.
visualDescription: A PC boot sequence diagram: power button → BIOS POST screen with hardware checks → OS loading screen, with the BIOS chip highlighted on the motherboard

quizQuestion: What does LAN stand for — the type of network that connects your home or office devices, typically using Ethernet cables or Wi-Fi?
explanation: LAN stands for Local Area Network. A LAN connects computers and devices within a limited geographic area (a home, office, or building). Ethernet and Wi-Fi are the dominant LAN technologies. The internet itself is the largest WAN (Wide Area Network), which interconnects millions of LANs worldwide.
visualDescription: A home office floor plan with computers, a printer, and a router all connected within the building, labeled 'LAN', with an arrow pointing outward labeled 'WAN = Internet'
